title: LLM Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of how large language models work — tokens, attention, context windows, and the training pipeline.

questions.question: What is the primary training objective of a large language model during pre-training?
options: Predict the next token in a sequence
Generate SQL queries from natural language
questions.explanation: LLMs are trained via self-supervised next-token prediction. Everything else — translation, coding, summarisation — emerges from doing this at massive scale.

questions.question: Approximately how many tokens does the sentence 'The quick brown fox' contain?
questions.explanation: Common English words are usually 1 token each. 'The', 'quick', 'brown', 'fox' = 4 tokens. The rule of thumb is 1 token ≈ 0.75 words.

questions.question: What is the computational complexity of the attention mechanism with respect to sequence length n?
questions.explanation: Standard self-attention is O(n²) in sequence length because every token attends to every other token. This is why long contexts cost significantly more compute.

questions.question: What does a KV cache store during transformer inference?
options: The model weights for fast loading
Key and value matrices from previously processed tokens
A cache of previous API responses
questions.explanation: The KV cache stores the key and value projections of previously seen tokens so the model doesn't recompute them on every generation step. It trades memory for speed.

questions.question: Which training stage teaches an LLM to follow instructions?
questions.explanation: Pre-training gives general language understanding. SFT on curated instruction/response pairs teaches the model to follow user instructions reliably.

questions.question: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem?
options: Tokens in the middle of a word are often split incorrectly
Models perform worse at recalling information placed in the middle of a long context
The model loses track of the conversation after 10 turns
Embeddings in the middle layers are less informative
questions.explanation: Research shows models recall information better from the start and end of a context window than from the middle. Critical information should be placed at the edges.
